content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
May 23,
1995
Brad Zerbe
6929 Lindero Lane
Rancho Murieta,
CA
95683
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-95-146
Dear Mr.
Zerbe:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding the campaign disclosure requirements of the Political
Reform Act
(the
“Act”) 1
Since you do not have the facts
pertaining to any specific “affinity” program the committee may
subscribe to,
but are requesting advice generally about such
programs,
we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance 2
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate ány conduct which may-have alreadytaken place.
In
addition,
this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3) .)
428 J STREET. SUITE 800. SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-95-146
Page
2
QUESTION
May a committee use an “affinity” program, as offered by
telephone companies as a means to raise funds for the committee?
CONCLUSION
A campaign may raise funds by use of an “affinity” program,
provided the procedures for solicitation and the reporting of
contributions comply with the provisions of the Act.
FACTS
You have asked whether a committee may use an “affinity”
program as a method to raise funds for the committee.
You stated
that affinity programs are offered by most major long distance
telephone service providers such as AT&T,
MCI,
U.S. Sprint,
and
Pacific Bell
(as well as many credit card companies)
as a
marketing device.
The way the program works is that the committee would sign up
for the affinity program with the telephone company.
The
telephone company would then advertise the committee’s involvement
as a means of soliciting new subscribers
(you submitted two
samples of the telephone company advertisements).
Individuals or
businesses subscribe for services from the telephone company with
the understanding that a percentage of their bill (usually three
percent)
is given by the telephone service to the committee.
You stated that the earmarked amount is then turned over to
the committee.
The telephone company also provides the
organization with the name,
address, phone number,
and amount
received from the subscriber.
-
ANALYSIS
-
We have previously advised that unusual methods of raising
funds,
such as the use of a 900—number telephone line to solicit
and receive contributions
(Rittenhouse Advice Letter,
No.
A-93-
189),
and a system whereby contributors could charge political
contributions to their credit card accounts by telephone
3 would
not be prohibited under the Act so long as all of the Act’s
requirements for recording and disclosure of contributions and
expenditures are satisfied.
In the Schwartz Advice Letter No. A-78-193,
we addressed a
system in which the contributor would make the telephone
contribution by calling a toll—free number shown on television
during a political commercial and would be connected with the
staff of a toll—free answering service who would record the amount
of the contribution and the caller’s credit card number.


Our File No.
1-95—146
Page3
For example:
o
The entire amount attributable to each subscriber that is
given by the telephone company to the committee is the amount of
the contribution.
o
It is assumed that the telephone company will not forward
the contribution to the committee until the caller pays the
telephone bill.
However,
when the payment is received by the
telephone company,
a contribution has been made.
Therefore, if
collection is to occur through the telephone company, the
telephone company will have to collect and provide to the campaign
in a timely fashion all information necessary for campaign
reporting, including the date of the contribution and required
information about the contribution.
(Buck-Walsh Advice Letter,
No.
1—90—239.)
o
You stated that the individual donors will generally not
provide $100 or more to the committee.
4
For reporting and
record]ceeping purposes, the committee is still required to
“maintain such detailed accounts, records, bills and receipts that
are necessary to prepare campaign statements.. ..“
Regulation
18401 provides:
(1)
For a contribution received or other
receipt of less than $25,
or an expenditure made of
less than $25:
-
(A)
The accounts and records shall
contain a continuous computation of campaign
account balances,
and also shall include a
listing reflecting the dates and daily totals
-
of such contributions, other receipts,
or
expenditures on the dates of such
contributions, other receipts or expenditures.
***
(2)
For a contribution received or other
receipt of $25 or more, but less than $100:
(A)
The accounts and records shall
contain all information required in
subdivision
(a) (1) (A)
above,
and shall include
the date of each contribution or other
receipt, the amount,
and the full name and
Section 84300(c)
provides that any contribution of $100 or more
must be made by written instrument containing the name of the
donor and the name of the payee.


Our File No.
1—95—146
Page
4
street address of the contributor or the
source of the other receipt.
In the case of a
contribution, the accounts and records also
shall contain the cumulative amount received
from the contributor and shall specify whether
the contribution is monetary or nonmonetary.
***
(3)
For a contribution received of $100 or
more:
(A)
The accounts and records shall
contain all information required in
subdivisions
(a) (1) (A)
and
(a) (2) (A)
above,
and shall include the occupation and employer
(or, if self-employed, the name of the
principal place of business)
of any
contributor.
o
Section 84302 requires that anyone acting as an
intermediary or agent of another in making a contribution of $100
or more disclose his or herown name, street address, occupation,
employer or, if self—employed, his or her principal place of
business and also disclose the same information as to the
contributor.
Information concerning both the intermediary and the
contributor must be disclosed on the committee’s campaign
statement.
However,
a person only qualifies as an intermediary if
the recipient of the contribution would consider the person to be
the contributor without the disclosure of the identity of the true
source of the contribution.
(Regulation 18432.5.)
This
definition would not apply under your facts.
-o
Any amounts retained by the telephone company to pay the
costs of the program are considered expenditures by the committee.
Therefore,
any amounts withheld by the telephone company must be
reported by the committee as expenditures on Schedule
E at the
time the fee is deducted.
o
You stated that in connection with the program, the
telephone company will advertise the committee’s involvement in
the program.
The committee will not pay for these services.
The
value of these services would not be considered a contribution to
the committee so long as the same services are provided to any
organization that is involved with the affinity program.
Section
82015 defines “contribution” to include the granting of discounts
or rebates not extended to the public generally.
(Section 82015.)


Our File No.
1—95-146
Page
5
Moreover, the Act would not require any specific disclaimers in
any advertisements sent by the telephone company.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—566O.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of lO per page.
Wallace
sel,
Legal Division

QUESTION: May a committee use an “affinity” program, as offered by
telephone companies as a means to raise funds for the committee?

CONCLUSION: A campaign may raise funds by use of an “affinity” program,
provided the procedures for solicitation and the reporting of
contributions comply with the provisions of the Act.
